quiz_questions.question_text: A matrix is singular if its determinant equals which value?

options.text: Infinity

quiz_questions.explanation: A singular matrix has a determinant of zero, which means it cannot be inverted.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is true about a non-singular matrix?

options.text: Its determinant is nonzero
It has no inverse
Its rows are linearly dependent
It maps every vector to the zero vector

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-singular matrix has a nonzero determinant, guaranteeing the existence of its inverse.

quiz_questions.question_text: If det(A) = 5, is matrix A singular or non-singular?

options.text: Both singular and non-singular
Non-singular
Cannot be determined
Singular

quiz_questions.explanation: Since det(A) = 5, which is nonzero, the matrix A is non-singular and therefore invertible.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which matrix is always non-singular?

options.text: A zero matrix
A matrix with a row of zeros
The identity matrix
A matrix with two identical columns

quiz_questions.explanation: The identity matrix always has a determinant of 1, making it non-singular regardless of its size.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a 3x3 matrix has two identical rows, what can you conclude?

options.text: The determinant equals 1
The matrix is non-singular
The matrix is singular
The matrix is diagonal

quiz_questions.explanation: Two identical rows are linearly dependent, forcing the determinant to zero and making the matrix singular.

quiz_questions.question_text: A non-singular matrix always has which property?

options.text: It is diagonal
It is symmetric
It has all positive entries
It has an inverse

quiz_questions.explanation: Every non-singular matrix is invertible, meaning a unique inverse matrix exists for it.

quiz_questions.question_text: For a 2x2 matrix [[a, b], [c, d]], when is it singular?

options.text: When a + d = 0
When ab - cd = 0
When ad + bc = 0
When ad - bc = 0

quiz_questions.explanation: The determinant of a 2x2 matrix is ad - bc, and the matrix is singular when this value equals zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many solutions does the system Ax = b have when A is singular and b is not in the column space of A?

options.text: Exactly one
No solution
Exactly two
Infinitely many

quiz_questions.explanation: When A is singular and b is outside the column space, the system is inconsistent and has no solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: If matrix A is non-singular, what is the rank of an n x n matrix A?

options.text: n
2n

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-singular n x n matrix has full rank n, meaning all its rows and columns are linearly independent.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which operation can turn a non-singular matrix into a singular one?

options.text: Swapping two rows
Multiplying a row by a nonzero scalar
Multiplying by the identity matrix
Replacing a row with a copy of another row

quiz_questions.explanation: Replacing a row with a duplicate of another creates linear dependence, making the determinant zero and the matrix singular.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a singular matrix?

options.text: A matrix whose transpose equals its inverse
A matrix that has only one row
A matrix with all entries equal to one
A square matrix whose determinant is zero and therefore has no inverse

quiz_questions.explanation: A singular matrix is a square matrix with determinant zero, which means no inverse exists for it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a non-singular matrix?

options.text: A matrix with more rows than columns
A matrix that cannot be multiplied
A matrix with all zero entries
A square matrix with a nonzero determinant that possesses a unique inverse

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-singular (invertible) matrix is a square matrix whose determinant is nonzero, guaranteeing the existence of a unique inverse.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the rank of a matrix?

options.text: The maximum number of linearly independent rows or columns in the matrix
The number of rows in the matrix
The sum of all its diagonal entries
The largest absolute value among its entries

quiz_questions.explanation: Rank measures the dimension of the column space (or row space), counting the maximum number of linearly independent rows or columns.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is linear dependence among rows of a matrix?

options.text: When at least one row can be expressed as a linear combination of the other rows
When the rows are arranged in increasing order
When the matrix has more columns than rows
When every row is a unit vector

quiz_questions.explanation: Rows are linearly dependent when at least one row can be written as a combination of the others, reducing the effective rank of the matrix.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the null space of a matrix?

options.text: The set of all eigenvalues of the matrix
The set of all vectors that the matrix maps to the zero vector
The set of entries that are zero in the matrix
The column space of the transposed matrix

quiz_questions.explanation: The null space (kernel) consists of all vectors x such that Ax = 0. For a singular matrix this space is nontrivial.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is full rank for a square matrix?

options.text: When every entry of the matrix is nonzero
When the rank equals the number of rows (and columns), making the matrix non-singular
When the matrix is upper triangular
When the rank equals half the number of rows

quiz_questions.explanation: An n x n matrix has full rank when its rank equals n, meaning all rows and columns are linearly independent.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a rank-deficient matrix?

options.text: A matrix whose rank is less than the minimum of its number of rows and columns
A matrix that has negative eigenvalues
A matrix with complex entries
A matrix that is not square

quiz_questions.explanation: A rank-deficient matrix has rank smaller than its theoretical maximum, indicating linear dependence among its rows or columns.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the determinant test for invertibility?

options.text: A matrix is invertible only if its trace is nonzero
A matrix is invertible only if its determinant equals 1
A matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is nonzero
A matrix is invertible only if its determinant is positive

quiz_questions.explanation: The determinant test states that a square matrix is invertible (non-singular) if and only if its determinant is not equal to zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the column space of a matrix?

options.text: The diagonal entries of the matrix
The set of all possible products of columns
The set of all linear combinations of the columns of the matrix
The set of all columns placed end to end

quiz_questions.explanation: The column space is the span of all column vectors and represents every vector b for which Ax = b has a solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for a matrix to be invertible?

options.text: It can be written as the product of two diagonal matrices
Its transpose is the zero matrix
There exists another matrix that, when multiplied with it, produces the identity matrix
It has more rows than columns

quiz_questions.explanation: A matrix is invertible if there is a matrix A^(-1) such that A * A^(-1) = A^(-1) * A = I, which requires a nonzero determinant.

quiz_questions.question_text: A structural engineer models a bridge with the system Ax = b, where A is a 4x4 matrix. She finds that det(A) = 0. What does this imply about the bridge forces?

options.text: The forces are uniquely determined according to conventional analysis
The bridge design is guaranteed to be safe within the typical range of values
The system has either no solution or infinitely many solutions, indicating the model is under-constrained
All forces equal zero, particularly regarding with as observed in standard experiments

quiz_questions.explanation: A singular coefficient matrix means the system lacks a unique solution, so the engineer cannot determine a single set of forces without additional constraints.

quiz_questions.question_text: A data scientist builds a regression model and discovers that two predictor columns in the design matrix are identical. What happens to the matrix?

options.text: The matrix becomes diagonal
The matrix becomes orthogonal
The matrix doubles in size
The matrix becomes singular due to perfect multicollinearity

quiz_questions.explanation: Identical columns create linear dependence, making the matrix singular and preventing unique coefficient estimation in regression.

quiz_questions.question_text: An electrical engineer writes the system of equations for a circuit as Ax = b, where A is 3x3 with det(A) = -7. Can she find unique currents?

options.text: No, because the determinant is negative
Only if all entries of b are positive
Yes, because A is non-singular and x = A^(-1)b gives the unique solution
No, because a 3x3 system always has infinitely many solutions

quiz_questions.explanation: Since det(A) = -7, which is nonzero, A is non-singular and the unique solution is obtained by multiplying both sides by A inverse.

quiz_questions.question_text: A robotics team programs an arm's transformation using matrix A. During testing, the arm collapses all 3D points onto a plane. What does this reveal about A?

options.text: A is the identity matrix
A is singular because it reduces the dimension of the output space
A has a determinant greater than 1
A is non-singular with full rank

quiz_questions.explanation: Mapping 3D space onto a 2D plane means the transformation loses a dimension, which only happens when the matrix is singular (rank-deficient).

quiz_questions.question_text: A professor gives students the matrix [[2, 4], [1, 2]] and asks them to classify it. What should they conclude?

options.text: It is non-singular because the trace is 4
It is singular because det = 2(2) - 4(1) = 0
It is non-singular because all entries are positive
It is singular because it has even numbers

quiz_questions.explanation: Computing the determinant: 2*2 - 4*1 = 0, so the matrix is singular and has no inverse.

quiz_questions.question_text: A chemical plant uses a 5x5 matrix to balance reaction equations. An analyst confirms it is non-singular. What can the plant conclude?

options.text: Multiple different balancing solutions exist
The reaction cannot proceed
The reaction is exothermic
There is exactly one set of balancing coefficients for the system

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-singular coefficient matrix guarantees a unique solution, so exactly one valid set of balancing coefficients exists.

quiz_questions.question_text: A game developer uses a 3x3 matrix to transform objects. She notices that after transformation, all objects appear flattened into a line. What is the rank of her matrix?
quiz_questions.explanation: Objects collapsing into a line means the image of the transformation is one-dimensional, so the matrix has rank 1 and is singular.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tries to compute A^(-1) for the matrix [[1, 2], [3, 6]] and gets an error. Which explanation is correct?

options.text: The software has a bug
Inverse computation only works for 3x3 or larger matrices
The matrix is singular since det = 1(6) - 2(3) = 0, so no inverse exists
The matrix is too small for inversion

quiz_questions.explanation: The determinant is 1*6 - 2*3 = 0, making the matrix singular. Singular matrices do not have inverses.

quiz_questions.question_text: A traffic network is modeled with a matrix equation Ax = b. Analysis shows A has a nontrivial null space. What does this mean for traffic flow?

options.text: No traffic can flow through the network
The network has exactly two solutions
There is exactly one traffic flow pattern
A is singular and there may be infinitely many valid flow patterns or none at all

quiz_questions.explanation: A nontrivial null space means A is singular, so the system either has infinitely many solutions or is inconsistent depending on b.

quiz_questions.question_text: An economics student multiplies two non-singular matrices A and B to form C = AB. Her professor asks: is C singular or non-singular?

options.text: C is non-singular only if A = B
C is non-singular because det(C) = det(A) * det(B), which is nonzero
C is always singular
C could be either singular or non-singular

quiz_questions.explanation: The product of two non-singular matrices is non-singular because det(AB) = det(A)*det(B), and the product of two nonzero numbers is nonzero.
